critic_raw:
This multi-spectral CNN for algal biomass prediction design is well-constructed in data hygiene but has critical problems in the label matching, spatial autocorrelation, and the implicit assumption about preprocessing independence.

In-situ sampling collects biomass measurements at specific geographic points (buoys, shore stations, transect locations), while satellite imagery covers the entire lake surface at pixel resolution. The label assignment assumes that the point measurement within ±2 days is representative of the spatial patch (64×64 or 128×128 pixels) used as CNN input. Algal bloom distributions are highly spatially heterogeneous — chlorophyll-a can vary by an order of magnitude over hundreds of meters within a single lake. If the in-situ sampling location does not correspond precisely to the spatial patch center, the label will represent a different location than the image input. The design selects patches 'centered on the sampling location or largest bloom region' but does not specify which criterion takes precedence or how the bloom region is identified without ground truth at inference time.

Images from the same lake collected in successive weeks are spatially correlated (same water body, same nutrient regime, same atmospheric conditions) and temporally correlated (bloom development is a continuous process). With training on years 1–3 and testing on year 5, images from a given lake in the training set and the test set share the same spatial autocorrelation structure. This does not constitute leakage in the strict sense, but it means that model performance on held-out years from the same lakes may be substantially more optimistic than model performance on new, unseen lakes. The design's generalizability claims are limited to the 8–12 observed lakes, but does not estimate how much of the performance would degrade on a new lake unseen in training.

The preprocessing claims 'atmospheric correction and radiometric calibration fit on training set only and applied to test set.' Atmospheric correction for satellite imagery typically uses physical models (e.g., MODTRAN, 6S radiative transfer) with parameters derived from the image itself (e.g., dark-pixel selection, aerosol optical depth estimation from image content). 'Fitting on training set' is not a well-defined concept for physical atmospheric correction algorithms — this may mean fitting normalization parameters after correction, but the claim conflates physically-parameterized correction with statistical preprocessing in a way that obscures what is actually being done and whether test-set statistics genuinely do not influence preprocessing.

The per-lake test MAE is listed as a secondary metric, but with only 1 year of test data per lake (year 5, summer only), each lake contributes at most a handful of image-label pairs to the per-lake MAE estimate, yielding unreliable per-lake performance estimates. Per-lake results cannot be used for meaningful generalization inferences without substantially more test observations per lake.
all_issues_raised: In-situ biomass point measurements are assigned to spatially extended image patches (64x128 pixels), but algal bloom chlorophyll-a can vary by an order of magnitude within a single lake; label representativeness depends on whether the sampling location aligns with the patch spatial footprint, which is neither guaranteed nor validated.

Images from the same 8-12 lakes appear in both training and test years, sharing spatial autocorrelation structure; performance on held-out years from observed lakes will be substantially more optimistic than performance on new unseen lakes, limiting the generalizability claim to the specific observed water bodies.

Atmospheric correction algorithms (MODTRAN, 6S) use parameters estimated from image content itself (aerosol optical depth, dark-pixel selection), making the claim that correction was 'fit on training data only' physically ambiguous; if test images contribute to their own atmospheric correction parameters, preprocessing is not truly leakage-free.

Per-lake secondary MAE estimates have insufficient support: with summer-only year 5 test data from 8-12 lakes, each lake contributes only a few image-label pairs to per-lake MAE, producing high-variance estimates that cannot support the stated generalization analysis.
all_issues_adjudicated: In-situ biomass point measurements are assigned to spatially extended image patches (64x128 pixels), but algal bloom chlorophyll-a can vary by an order of magnitude within a single lake; label representativeness depends on whether the sampling location aligns with the patch spatial footprint, which is neither guaranteed nor validated.

Images from the same 8-12 lakes appear in both training and test years, sharing spatial autocorrelation structure; performance on held-out years from observed lakes will be substantially more optimistic than performance on new unseen lakes, limiting the generalizability claim to the specific observed water bodies.

Atmospheric correction algorithms (MODTRAN, 6S) use parameters estimated from image content itself (aerosol optical depth, dark-pixel selection), making the claim that correction was 'fit on training data only' physically ambiguous; if test images contribute to their own atmospheric correction parameters, preprocessing is not truly leakage-free.
